questions.text: The terms 'Socialist' and 'Secular' were added to the Preamble of the Indian Constitution by which Amendment?
questions.explanation: The 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976 added 'Socialist', 'Secular', and upgraded 'unity' to 'unity and integrity' of the Nation.
questions.source: Confirmed recurring SSC CGL question format

questions.text: Before the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976, the term 'secular' was mentioned only in which Article of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: Article 25 (freedom of religion) was the only place 'secular' appeared before 1976, even though the Preamble itself didn't use the word until the 42nd Amendment.
questions.source: Confirmed recurring SSC CGL question format

questions.text: The idea of the Preamble of the Indian Constitution was borrowed from which country's Constitution?
questions.explanation: The opening words 'WE, THE PEOPLE OF...' and the very concept of a Preamble were adapted from the U.S. Constitution.
questions.source: Appears frequently in SSC CGL test banks — exact year/paper not independently confirmed

questions.text: Which Supreme Court case held that the Preamble is NOT a part of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: The Berubari Union case (1960) held the Preamble is not part of the Constitution. This was reversed 13 years later by the Kesavananda Bharati case (1973) — a classic 'which case said what, and which one reversed it' trap.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated based on a well-known reversal pattern

questions.text: In which year was the Kesavananda Bharati case decided, establishing that the Preamble IS part of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973) — also the case that introduced the Basic Structure doctrine.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — frequently glossed over but easy to test as a standalone date

questions.text: Which 1995 Supreme Court case reaffirmed that the Preamble is part of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: The LIC of India case (1995) reaffirmed the Kesavananda Bharati position on the Preamble.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — a lesser-known follow-up case worth knowing

questions.text: How many times has the Preamble of the Indian Constitution been amended so far?
questions.explanation: Only once — by the 42nd Amendment Act, 1976. A very commonly tested 'true or false' style fact.
questions.source: Pattern frequently recurring across SSC CGL/CHSL polity papers in various phrasings

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT one of the four ideals mentioned in the Preamble?
questions.explanation: The four ideals are Justice, Liberty, Equality, and Fraternity. 'Sovereignty' is a descriptive word about the State, not one of the four ideals/goals.
questions.source: Pattern frequently recurring — tests whether students can distinguish 'descriptive words' from 'ideals' in the Preamble
